summary: Gruber responds to Steve Jobs's announcement of another medical leave of absence from Apple in January 2011. He notes the strategic timing of the announcement on a market holiday, just before Apple's expected blockbuster quarterly earnings. He argues that Apple is better prepared than ever to weather Jobs's absence, pointing to the company's deep executive bench and its successful track record during Jobs's 2009 leave. He pushes back against media narratives suggesting Apple needs to formally transfer power, arguing the company already functions well without Jobs at the helm daily.
key_points: The timing of Jobs's leave announcement was strategically chosen: a market holiday followed by expected record-breaking quarterly earnings
Apple's growth trajectory is staggering — from $1.38B revenue in Q4 2001 to an expected $26B in Q4 2010
Apple proved during Jobs's 2009 absence that the product cycle continues without him, with new products released and future ones progressed
Media narratives calling for formal power transfers misunderstand how Apple already operates
money_quotes: Whatever we expected, we all hoped we'd seen our last Steve Jobs medical leave. When the news went out this morning, there was a collective, wincing "Fuck."

The company has never been in better shape than it is today. Hit products in growing markets, best-of-breed technology, top-notch engineers and designers, thriving retail stores, and the strongest brand in the history of consumer electronics.
Nobody is ever truly ready for news like this, but you can be prepared. Apple is.
Was it the same without him? No, of course not. Did the company function just fine? Yes.
Jobs's leave of absence is cause for concern — and for good thoughts and well-wishes. It is not cause for panic.
key_insight: Apple's institutional strength and deep executive bench mean Jobs's medical leave is cause for concern but not panic, as the company proved during his 2009 absence that it can execute without him.
